---
title: "Blog"
title-block-style: none
pagetitle: "Blog | Mesfin Genie"
description: "Research notes and teaching explainers by Dr Mesfin Genie."
page-layout: full
image: /assets/img/og-card.png
---

```{=html}
<header class="page-header"><div class="page-shell"><p class="eyebrow">Blog</p><h1>Research notes and teaching explainers</h1><p class="lead">Substantive posts on health economics, choice modelling, behavioural evidence and policy translation.</p></div></header><section class="content-section"><div class="page-shell grid-3"><article class="post-card"><div class="meta">2026-07-02</div><h3><a href="/blog/posts/the-benchmark-before-bias/">The benchmark before bias</a></h3><p>Why behavioural economics starts with rational choice rather than replacing it.</p></article><article class="post-card"><div class="meta">2026-07-02</div><h3><a href="/blog/posts/editorial-calendar/">Editorial calendar for behavioural economics and health-policy posts</a></h3><p>Planned themes for future research notes and teaching explainers.</p></article><article class="post-card"><div class="meta">2026-07-01</div><h3><a href="/blog/posts/why-99-does-not-feel-like-100/">Why 99% does not feel like 100%</a></h3><p>The certainty effect, Allais-style choices and why guaranteed outcomes receive special treatment.</p></article><article class="post-card"><div class="meta">2026-06-28</div><h3><a href="/blog/posts/what-a-discrete-choice-experiment-measures/">What a discrete choice experiment actually measures</a></h3><p>A plain guide to what a DCE can tell you, and what it cannot.</p></article><article class="post-card"><div class="meta">2026-06-21</div><h3><a href="/blog/posts/averages-hide-the-people-who-matter/">Averages hide the people who matter</a></h3><p>Why preference heterogeneity, not the average, is often the real finding.</p></article><article class="post-card"><div class="meta">2026-06-14</div><h3><a href="/blog/posts/what-eye-tracking-reveals/">What eye tracking reveals about how we choose</a></h3><p>Using eye tracking to see how people read a choice task, and why it matters.</p></article><article class="post-card"><div class="meta">2026-06-07</div><h3><a href="/blog/posts/does-feedback-help-people-choose/">Does feedback help people make better choices?</a></h3><p>Giving respondents feedback in a preference task can guide them, or mislead them.</p></article><article class="post-card"><div class="meta">2026-05-31</div><h3><a href="/blog/posts/preference-research-and-vaccine-mandates/">What preference research adds to the vaccine mandate debate</a></h3><p>Mandates are usually argued in absolutes. Preference research shows the trade-offs.</p></article><article class="post-card"><div class="meta">2026-05-24</div><h3><a href="/blog/posts/why-i-build-decision-tools/">Why I build decision tools</a></h3><p>Turning preference studies into tools that decision-makers can actually use.</p></article></div></section>
```
